Reviewer • COC 3 — Computer Systems Servicing NC II

COC 3 — Set-Up Computer Server

This reviewer covers the complete step-by-step process for setting up a Windows Server 2008 R2 computer server — from initial IP configuration and server roles, through DNS, DHCP, File Services, and folder redirection with file screening.

COC 3 Server Roles to Install
Active Directory Domain Services (AD DS)
DNS Server — Domain Name System
DHCP Server — Dynamic Host Configuration
File Services + Folder Redirection + File Screening
Print and Document Services
Remote Desktop Services
Review Progress0%

This is your complete reviewer for Core Competency 3 — Set-Up Computer Server. Follow the steps in order. The checklist tables throughout show which roles have been completed (✓) versus pending.

Make sure COC 2 → Install & Configure is complete before starting this. Note: Do NOT create Users or Organizational Units if the required roles are not yet installed.

Client Server Network Design

Qualification: Computer Systems Servicing NC II — Core Competency: Set-Up Computer Server

Network Topology Overview

The network design shows a Desktop client, a Server, a Switch, a Router (range 10–30), Wi-Fi Protected Access, an Access Point, a Keystone, Modular Box, and a Patch Panel all interconnected in a client-server architecture.

Client Server Network Design — Desktop, Server, Switch, Router, Access Point, Patch Panel

Server Setup Checklist

Complete all items in order — check each before proceeding to roles

⚠️
Note: Checklist must be done before Adding Roles

All items in the initial checklist must be completed and verified before you proceed to the Patch Panel and adding server roles.

Pre-Setup Checklist Item YESNO
Windows Server 2008 R2 License Activated✓—
Router is properly configured with range 10–30✓—
Wi-Fi Protected Access configured✓—
Windows Server I.P. Address is set static according to reference✓—
Server Computer is named "Server"✓—
Enable Remote Desktop set to Allow✓—
1

Initial Server Setup

Steps 1–5: IP configuration, static address, rename server, enable remote desktop, add roles

  1. 1
    Check your I.P. Address Press ⊞ + R for "RUN", type cmd and run ipconfig to see your current IP address.
  2. 2
    Set the Server I.P. Address to Static Given the IP Address, set the server I.P. Address to static (do not use DHCP — it must be fixed).
  3. 3
    Verify Server is Fully Activated with an IP Address Check that your server is fully activated and has a valid IP address assigned.
Steps 1–3: CMD ipconfig (Figure 1), Set Static IP (Figure 2), Verify Activation (Figure 3)
  1. 4
    Rename Computer to "Server1" & Enable Remote Desktop Go to Computer Properties, rename the server to "Server1", and enable Remote Desktop — select "Allow connections from computers running any version of Remote Desktop (less secure)".
  2. 5
    Open Initial Configuration Task — Click "Add Roles" Press Start Button and type oobe for the Initial Configuration Task to pop up. Click on "Add Roles".
Step 4: Enable Remote Desktop (Figure 4) — Step 5: Add Roles via Initial Configuration Task (Figure 5)
2

Set-Up Computer Server Roles

Install all 6 roles in order — track progress with the checklist below

Set-up Computer Server Roles StepsYESNO
1. Active Directory Domain Services☐☐
2. DNS Server☐☐
3. DHCP Server☐☐
4. File Services☐☐
5. Print and Document Services☐☐
6. Remote Desktop Services☐☐
AD

Installing Active Directory Domain Services (AD DS)

Role 1 — Step 6

What is AD DS?

Active Directory Domain Services stores information about objects on the network and makes this information available to users and network administrators. AD DS uses domain controllers to give network users access to permitted resources anywhere on the network.

  1. 6
    Click "Add Role" — Select Active Directory Domain Services The Add Role Wizard will appear. Click on Active Directory Domain Services, then add .Net Framework 3.5.1 when prompted. Follow the installation setup by pressing Next and Install Button.
Step 6 — Add Roles Wizard: Select Active Directory Domain Services & add .NET Framework 3.5.1 (Figure 6)
After Installation

Check the report information to confirm the installation was a success before proceeding to the next role.

DNS

Installing DNS Server

Role 2 — Steps 7–11

DNS Setup via dcpromo

The DNS Server is installed through the AD DS promotion wizard using the dcpromo command. This configures the Domain Name System client settings automatically.

  1. 7
    Type "dcpromo" — Follow DNS Server Setup Press Start Button and on the search tab type dcpromo. Follow the DNS Server set-up installation. Check the second checkbox — "Automatically correct the problem by installing DNS Server service on this computer".
  2. 8
    Select "Create new Domain Forest" — Hit Next On the Domain Forest screen, select Create new Domain Forest and click Next.
  3. 9
    Provide the Server Domain Name Enter your domain name by adding the following domain register format: domainname.com / .net / .org etc.
  4. 10
    Set Forest Functional Level to Windows Server 2008 R2 Select Windows Server 2008 R2 as the Forest Functional Level.
Steps 7–10 — DNS Setup: dcpromo wizard, Figure 7 (DNS checkbox), Figure 8 (Domain Forest)
  1. 11
    Check "Reboot on Completion" to Finalize DNS Server Setup At the end of the wizard, check Reboot on Completion so the server restarts automatically to finalize the DNS Server setup.
Step 11 — Check "Reboot on completion" to finalize DNS Server Setup (Figure 11)
DHCP

Installing DHCP Server

Role 3 — Steps 12–15

📡
DHCP Server Setup

Wait for the Initial Configuration Task to appear again after reboot, then Hit Add Role again for DHCP Server Setup. Check the DHCP Server option in the Add Role Wizard.

  1. 12
    Add Role → Select DHCP Server Wait for the Initial Configuration Task to appear. Hit Add Role again for DHCP Server Setup. Check the DHCP Server box in the Add Role Wizard.
  2. 13
    Enter Server I.P. Address — Validate DNS Following the Installation Set-up, enter the Server I.P. Address for the DHCP Server. Click Validate to confirm it is valid (should show ✅ Valid).
  3. 14
    Add Scope — Set Domain Name & IP Range Put the domain name in the scope name and type .scope as its extension name. Starting I.P. Address and End should be the same as the router's range and default gateway.
  4. 15
    Select "Disable DHCPv6" Throughout Installation As you progress through the installation, select Disable DHCPv6 when prompted.
Steps 12–15 — DHCP Server: Add Role, Validate IP (Figure 13), Add Scope (Figure 14)
FS

Installing File Services

Role 4 — Step 16

  1. 16
    Add Role → File Services → Check FILE RESOURCE MANAGER By adding role again, click on File Services and proceed as the installation continues. Check the FILE RESOURCE MANAGER and select the partition you want to use for data to be saved (Local Disk D:).
Step 16 — File Services: Roles checklist (4 installed) and Add Role Wizard (Figures 16–18)
File Services: Select NTFS volume for data storage — Local Disk D: checked (Figure 18)
📌
Summary — 4 Roles Installed

After File Services is complete, 4 roles are installed. These roles will be carried over before doing folder redirection. Do NOT create Users or Organizational Units yet if these roles are not fully installed.

File Redirection — Users & Folder Redirection

Complete all 7 steps before proceeding to remaining server roles

⚠️
Before you proceed to Roles 5 and 6, complete the following setup first:
Users and Folder Redirection StepsYESNO
1. Create Organizational Unit (Shared Folder)☐☐
2. Create Users☐☐
3. Folder Redirection☐☐
4. Log In Computer Clients☐☐
5. Create File Groups☐☐
6. File Screening☐☐
7. Proceed to Server Role Steps☐☐

Step 16 — Create a Shared Folder (Organizational Unit)

  • In File Services, the selected drive is Local Disk D:
  • Right Click the folder → select Sharing tab → click Share → check "Share this folder" box
  • Make sure you select Everyone on the drop-down menu → Add → make it Read/Write
  • Click on Advanced Sharing and check if it's on full control → click "Share this folder"
Step 17 — File Sharing: Set Everyone to Read/Write permissions (Figure 17)

Step 18 — Create Organizational Unit & Users in Active Directory

  • Press Start → drag mouse to Administrative Tools → select Active Directory Users and Computers
  • Expand your css.com domain name and right-click your DNS to add your Organizational Unit
  • New → Organizational Unit — name it same as your shared folder (e.g. "Organizationalunit1")
  • A folder will appear in the list of subfolders — create users inside that folder
  • Fill up user information e.g. User1 — this user will be your log-in account on client computers
Step 18 — Administrative Tools → Active Directory Users and Computers (Figure 18)
Step 18.1 — Right-click Organizational Unit → New → User (Figure 18.1)

Folder Redirection via Group Policy

Steps 19 — Link GPO to Organizational Unit and redirect folders

Group Policy Management

You can add group policy. Press Start → Administrative Tools → Group Policy Management. Inside Group Policy Management window, expand Forest: css.com → Domains → DNS → you can now view your created Organizational Unit.

  1. 19
    Create a GPO in Organizational Unit → Link it Here Right-click on your Organizational Unit folder → first selection: "Create a GPO in this domain, and Link it here…". After this, rename this link same with your Organizational Unit — a link will appear in selections. Right-click on this and select Edit.
Step 19.1 — Group Policy Management: Create GPO in Organizationalunit1 (Figure 19.1)
  • Navigate: User Configuration → Policies → Windows Settings → Folder Redirection
  • Get the Network Path of your Shared folder in Local Disk — e.g. \SERVER\Organizationalunit1
  • Right-click each folder → Properties → Settings → enter the full network path location
  • Setting: "Basic - Redirect everyone's folder to the same location"
  • Root Path: \SERVER\Organizationalunit1
  • Start Menu will be different — Redirect to the following location same network path to resume Folder Redirection
Step 19.6 — AppData(Roaming) Properties: Basic redirect to \SERVER\Organizationalunit1 (Figure 19.6)
Result

If you follow these steps you may proceed to logging in your user accounts and linking each client's computer to your server. A folder will appear from your Shared folder in D: Drive.

File Screening

Step 20 — Block specific file types from user accounts using File Server Resource Manager

File Server Resource Manager

This program allows you to block specific file types from your user accounts. The format for adding patterns is *.FileExtension

  1. 20
    Start → Administrative Tools → File Server Resource Manager Navigate to File Screening → File Groups. You can block file extensions here. Click on Create File Group to create a custom File Group. The format of adding pattern is *.FileExtension
Step 20 — File Server Resource Manager: File Groups → Create File Group (Figure 20)
